coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots/1D MoO3-x hybrid junction with enhanced LED light efficiency in photocatalytic inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yeping
Wang, Chaobao
Huang, Liying
Zhang, Fei
Wang, Hao
Wang, Qian
Yang, Lei
Xie, Meng
Gan, Yu
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Novel carbon quantum dots/1D MoO3-x hybrid junction (noted as CQDs/MoO3-x) was obtained by a facile hydrothermal route. The CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was estimated as an effective photocatalyst for inactivating E. coli and S. aureus under LED light. Compared with pure MoO3 and MoO3-x, CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst displayed better performance in inactivating E. coli and S. aureus. The results of UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–vis DRS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) indicated the enhanced photoactivity is ascribed to: (1) MoO3-x has good absorption property of visible light; (2) the 1D structure of MoO3-x prolongs the lifetime of photo-excited charge carriers; (3) CQDs modifying 1D MoO3-x is not only favorable for absorption of visible light, but also for the fast transport and surface separation of the charge carriers. The capture experiment showed that holes (h+) are main active species in the photocatalytic process. This work presents an efficient photocatalyst for water antibacterial treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen vacancy
1D MoO3-x
CQDs
Photocatalytic
LED light

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis process: CQDs/MoO3-x.
(a) XRD patterns of MoO3, MoO3-x and CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts, (b) enlarged XRD patterns between 20 and 34°.

(a) TEM image of MoO3-x; (b) and (c): TEM images of 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst; (d) electron diffraction patterns of the area noted by yellow circle in (a); (e) and (f): magnified TEM images of the area noted by yellow circle in (b) and (c). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FT-IR spectra of MoO3, MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst.
(a) XPS survey spectra of MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst; high-resolution XPS spectra: (b) C1s, (c) Mo3d for 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst and (d) O1s for MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x.
ESR spectra of MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x.

Bactericidal efficiency against (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus; bacterial colony images (c) E. coli and (d) S. aureus at different irradiation times under LED light; the samples are noted as a: 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, b: 5% CQDs/MoO3-x, c: 1% CQDs/MoO3-x, d: MoO3-x, e: MoO3, f: No catalyst, g: No light.
LSCM images of E. coli being irradiated under LED light by the 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst for (a) 0 min, (b) 10 min, (c) 20 min, (d) 30 min and (e) 40 min.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of MoO3, MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3 photocatalyst; (b) band gap of MoO3-x; (c) Mott-Schottky plots of MoO3-x.

(a) EIS spectra of MoO3, MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst; (b) enlarged EIS spectra of MoO3, MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst from the circle in Fig. 10a, (c) trapping experiments by inactivating E. coli for 3% CQDs/MoO3-x.
Proposed mechanism in photocatalytic system for CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots/1D MoO3-x hybrid junction with enhanced LED light efficiency in photocatalytic inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel carbon quantum dots/1D MoO3-x hybrid junction (noted as CQDs/MoO3-x) was obtained by a facile hydrothermal route. The CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was estimated as an effective photocatalyst for inactivating E. coli and S. aureus under LED light. Compared with pure MoO3 and MoO3-x, CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst displayed better performance in inactivating E. coli and S. aureus. The results of UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–vis DRS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) indicated the enhanced photoactivity is ascribed to: (1) MoO3-x has good absorption property of visible light; (2) the 1D structure of MoO3-x prolongs the lifetime of photo-excited charge carriers; (3) CQDs modifying 1D MoO3-x is not only favorable for absorption of visible light, but also for the fast transport and surface separation of the charge carriers. The capture experiment showed that holes (h+) are main active species in the photocatalytic process. This work presents an efficient photocatalyst for water antibacterial treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increase of population and the discharge of pollutants, the environmental pollution caused by microorganisms is becoming more and more serious. According to the survey of the world health organization (WHO), 80% of human infectious diseases are related to drinking water polluted by microorganisms, so sterilization technology is urgently needed. Recently, effective antibacterial strategies, such as chlorination [1], ozonation [2], reactive oxygen species [3], UV irradiation and photocatalytic disinfection [4] have been studied as antibacterial application. Among above methods, photocatalytic sterilization is an effective method, because photocatalyst has the advantages of feasible synthesis, low cost and being applied under solar light irradiation [5–7]. To design high efficient photocatalytic antibacterial materials, lots of antibacterial materials, such as, ZnO-based [8,9], Bi2O3-based [10,11], g–C3N4–based [12], ZnFe2O4 [13] and TiO2/ZnCr2O4 [14] have been successfully designed.

MoO3-x, as a typical n type of semiconductor, has been widely applied in CO2 reduction [15], lithium storage [16], supercapacitors [17], hydrogen evolution [18,19], NIR-mediated photothermal therapy (PTT)/photodynamic therapy (PDT) of cancer [20,21]. Compared with MoO3, MoO3-x possesses a narrower band gap, better adsorption property of visible light and electrical conductivity [17,21]. The oxygen deficient structure in MoO3-x is able to facilitate charge transfer and narrowing bandgaps in catalytic reactions [22]. Nevertheless, there are potential drawbacks for MoO3-x: (1) a chemical instability material, O2 and/or H2O are usually adsorbed on the oxygen vacancies of MoO3-x and subsequently turn MoO3-x into MoO3 [23], (2) although the layered structure of MoO3-x provided the electrolyte ions with unimpeded highway, the charge transfer between adjacent MoO3-x nano units still remain as an obstacle in improving its high-rate photoactivity [24,25]. To overcome the problems, hybrid MoO3-x composites were synthesized. For example, MoO3-x/graphene nanoflake hybrid nanomaterials was designed for photoluminescence properties [26]. MoNi4/MoO3-x nanorod was synthesized for highly efficient hydrogen evolution [27]. Silver-MoO3-x was applied in solar cells [28] and antibacterial [29]. Ru–MoO3 - x/C was fabricated for high-efficient preparation of gasoline-ranged C5–C6 [30]. It is documented that MoO3-x-based composites showed better performance in catalytic reactions compared with pure MoO3. The research on the improvement of photoactivity and photocatalytic application of MoO3-x system has not been investigated in detail. In addition, the microstructure of materials played an important role in enhancing the properties of semiconductors [31]. In various microstructures, one-dimensional structure presented an effective electron transport path, which can improve the lifetime of photo generated carriers [31,32]. Based on above investigation about MoO3-x, it is an interesting work for us to design highly efficient photocatalytic material on the base of 1D MoO3-x.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been attracted much attention for their chemical inertness, easy functionalization, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [33]. It can be used in many ways, such as medical [34], energy storage [35], solar energy conversion [36] and photocatalytic application [37,38]. Because of π-system of CQDs, it presented photoinduced electron-transfer and electron reservoir properties, which can be viewed as a favorable surface modified material. The photocatalytic activity of conventional semiconductors were improved via being combined with CQDS [37]. The advantages of CQDs inspires us to deposit CQDs on 1D MoO3-x nanocrystals. If the hybrid material CQDs/MoO3-x is obtained, the better visible light absorption, enhanced transferring efficiency of electron-hole pairs and high photocatalytic activity will be anticipated. As far as we know, it is scarcely reported on the synthesis and investigation of such a CQDs/MoO3-x hybrid material and application in antibacterial.

In the study, CQDs/MoO3-x was prepared by a facile hydrothermal reaction. The obtained photocatalyst was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR), electron spin resonance (ESR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) and Laser Confocal Microscopy (LSCM) respectively. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/MoO3-x was detected by the photo-catalytic antibacterial performance on E. coli and S. aureus. It is found that the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst exhibits significantly improved photocatalytic activity compared with 1D MoO3-x nanobelts. Moreover, the photocatalytic mechanism for efficient enhancement photoactivity is proposed according to trapping experiment and energy band structure.

Honey was from Zhoushi Shunfa Foodstuff Co., Ltd. Guilin. The other reagents were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd and used without further purification. The dialysis bag was made in MYM Biological Technology Company Limited. The typical synthesis route of CQDs is as follows: 2.0 g honey, 8 mL ultrapure water and 2 mL 30% H2O2 were mixed thoroughly in a beaker. Then, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave and then heated at 140 °C for 2 h. When the autoclave completely cooled to room temperature, the pH value of the suspension liquid was tuned to 7 by dropping NaOH solution (2 mol·L−1). Subsequently, the larger particles were removed by 0.22 μm filtration membrane. Finally, the CQDs powder was obtained after being purified thoroughly for 2 days using filter membrane (MDCO: 1000 D) and following freeze-drying.

The CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was prepared by a mild hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 1 ). First, the 1D MoO3 nanobelts were synthesized according to the literature [39]. Second, 500 mg MoO3 was put into 15 mL anhydrous ethanol, which contained a certain amount of CQDs solution (2 mg·L−1). After continuous ultrasonic treatment for 2 h, the mixture was transferred into 25 mL autoclave, which was then sealed and heated at 140 °C for 12 h in a drying oven. When it completely cooled to room temperature, the sediment was collected and washed by deionized water and anhydrous ethanol for several times. Finally, the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was obtained after being dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The mass content of CQDs added in the CQDs/MoO3-x was 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt% respectively. The samples were named as 1% CQDs/MoO3-x, 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, 5% CQDs/MoO3-x. MoO3-x was obtained in the similar way as CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst without CQDs.
Detailed characterization and electrochemical experiments were shown in the supporting material. The information of LED light was provided in Fig. S1 and Table S1.

Antibacterial activity of the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was detected by deactivation of E. coli and S. aureus. At the beginning of experiment, both the beaker and the culture medium need to be sterilized in the sterilization pot at 121 °C for 30 min. First of all, E. coli cell was mixed into 3.0 mL Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium, and then the solution was transferred into a 37 °C in a water bath thermostat shaker. After being shaken for 18 h, the E. coli cell solution was adjusted to 107 CFU/mL by 0.9% NaCl solution.

Briefly, 30 mg of CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was put into 50 mL suspension containing E. coli cell and stirred for 30 min against exposure to light. Then the mixed suspension was sucked out at 0 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, and 30 min, respectively. At each time, 100 μL suspension was quickly spread to agar plate and incubated in mold incubator for 18 h at 37 °C. The antibacterial efficiency percent was calculated by the following Eq. (1)
(1)
Antibacterial efficiency
( % )
=
( 1 -
N/N 0
)
∗ 100

N0 is initial amount of the bacterial at 0 min and N is final amount in the agar plate, respectively. The cycle experiments were designed as follows: at the end of each cycle, the photocatalysts were obtained by centrifuging. After the supernatant was removed, 50 ml 107 CFU/mL E. coli containing of fresh saline was added to a glass reactor for the next bacterial inactivation cycle.

The structure of MoO3, MoO3-x and CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts was measured by XRD patterns (Fig. 2 ). For MoO3, the main diffraction peaks of photocatalysts can be observed at 12.7°, 23.3°, 25.7°, 27.3°, 33.7°, 35.4°, 39.0°, 46.2°, 49.2°, 54.1°, 55.1◦and 58.9°, which can be attributed to the (020), (110), (040), (021), (111), (041), (060), (210), (002), (221), (112) and (081) crystal planes of MoO3-x (JCPDS No. 5–0508) [40], respectively. For MoO3-x, the peaks are in agreement with those of MoO3. For CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts, the main peaks of MoO3-x are included (Fig. 2a), while the splitting peaks at (110), (040), (021) planes are observed in Fig. 2b (enlarged spectra from Fig. 2a). The result is due to the presence of Mo2O5(OH) (JCPDS No. 76–1681) in the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts. In addition, the peaks of CQDs cannot be found in the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts, which was attributed to the low content of CQDs in the photocatalyst. This phenomenon is similar with previous literature [41,42].

The morphology of MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst is shown by SEM (Fig. S2). It can be observed that MoO3-x was 1D belts structure with the width from 0.18 μm to 0.58 μm, length from 4.00 μm to 9.02 μm (Fig. S2a). For 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, most of MoO3-x belts are similar with pure MoO3-x, which indicated that the CQDs did not influence the morphology of MoO3-x in photocatalyst process (Fig. S2b). Meanwhile, the result of EDS mapping exhibited that 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst contains Mo, O, and C elements (Fig. S3).

The morphology of the MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst is further observed by TEM (Fig. 3 ). 1D MoO3-x is observed with 1D belts morphology (Fig. 3a). The selected area electron diffraction patterns were measured on 1D MoO3-x (yellow circle from Fig. 3a) and shown in Fig. 3d. The diffraction crystal planes (202), (004) and (−202) indicated the crystal structure of MoO3-x. In addition, the HRTEM image of 1D MoO3-x sample from the TEM image (Fig. S4a) is shown in Fig. S4b, where the lattice spacing of 0.346 nm can be assigned to the (040) plane [40]. The analysis matches with the result of XRD. For 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, the TEM images are shown in Fig. 3b, c, e and f. It can be observed some CQDs were evenly attached on the surface of MoO3-x nanobelts, indicating the formation of hybrid interface in CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst.

To further verify the structure of CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, the samples were detected by FT-IR (Fig. 4 ). For the MoO3, the absorption bands at 561, 879, and 999 cm−1 are assigned to stretching modes of Mo–O–Mo and MoO stretching mode, respectively [43]. For MoO3-x, the stretching modes of Mo–O–Mo and MoO are also clearly observed. For 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, Mo–O–Mo and MoO absorption peaks were observed at 561, 879, and 999 cm−1, which is in agreement with that of MoO3. In addition, the absorption bands at 2923 and 3430 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of C–H and the stretching vibration of O–H, respectively [44–46]. The result confirms the presence of CQDs and indicates that CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst is successfully synthesized.

XPS measurement was taken to investigate chemical composition of MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x. The survey spectra are shown in Fig. 5 a. Obviously, pure MoO3-x contains Mo and O elements and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst contains Mo, C and O elements. For the 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, three peaks at 284.4, 285.3 and 287.2 eV belonged to the C–C/CC, oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon, respectively (Fig. 5 b) [47,48]. Mo3d and O1s fitting spectra were taken to detect the surface oxygen vacancy and oxidation state of MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x. For MoO3-x, the binding energies at 235.7 and 232.5 eV are assigned to Mo6+, while the peaks at 234.3 and 231.2 eV corresponded to Mo5+ [29,40]. Given the peak area of the Mo 3d XPS spectra, the ratio of Mo5+ to Mo6+ is 5:95 in MoO3-x. In the case of 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, the binding energies of Mo6+ was at 236.0 and 232.8 eV and the binding energies of Mo5+ was at 234.8 and 231.4 eV (Fig. 5c), showing a slight positive shift compared with that of MoO3-x. For MoO3-x, the O1s peaks at 531.1 eV and 530.5 eV originated from oxygen vacancy and the lattice oxygen in MoO3, respectively [26]. In the case of 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, two O1s peaks are observed at 531.1 eV and 530.2 eV, which correspond to oxygen vacancies and Mo–O in MoO3-x, respectively [49] (Fig. 5d). The result of XPS indicates that the interaction exists between CQDs and MoO3-x.

The oxygen vacancy of MoO3-x and CQDs/MoO3-x was further investigated by ESR experiment (Fig. 6 ) [50]. For MoO3-x, a typical peak at g = 2.0000 is clearly found, which is due to the unpaired electron being captured in oxygen vacancy [51,52]. Nevertheless, for 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, the oxygen vacancy signal is weaker than that of MoO3-x, which may be attributed to the combination of CQDs. Therefore, based on the analysis of Mo3d, O1s and ESR spectra, oxygen vacancy was confirmed in MoO3-x and CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst.

The antibacterial performance of MoO3-x and CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst were investigated by inactivating E. coli and S. aureus. The antibacterial efficiency was estimated by the suspension of bacteria and photocatalysts for designed irradiating time (0, 10, 20, 30,40 min). In Fig. 7 a and b, the dark control or no photocatalyst experiment did not influence the viable cell density, which indicated the as-prepared photocatalysts or the LED light were nontoxic on the bacterial. For MoO3-x, only 70.5% E. coli was inactivated within 30 min. In the case of CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, the samples exhibit better antibacterial activity than MoO3-x. Remarkably, the optimum sample 3% CQDs/MoO3-x can inactivate 99.8% of E. coli within 30 min (Fig. 7a). Similarly, CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts showed better antibacterial performance than pure MoO3-x in inactivating S. aureus. For the MoO3-x, 56.5% S. aureus can be inactivated within 20 min. 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst showed the optimum inactivation activity (Fig. 7b), which can inactivate 99.5% S. aureus within 20 min. In the meantime, the 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst showed better inactivation activity than the 3% CQDs/MoO3 (Figs. S5a and 5b), the results showed that oxygen vacancy was helpful to increase the photocatalytic activity. The bacterial colony images of MoO3, MoO3-x and CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst were presented in Fig. 7c and d. After being loaded with CQDs, improved antibacterial efficiency was obtained on all CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalysts under LED-light illumination. Based on the result of photocatalytic performance, oxygen vacancy and the interaction between CQDs and MoO3-x both contribute to the enhanced antibacterial activity.

To further evaluate the inactivation effect of the CQDs/MoO3-x composites on E. coli, LSCM was applied to verify the change of bacteria viability with time. It is well known that the SYTO 9 only can stain living bacterial and gives out green fluorescence, while SYTO 9 and PI can stain dead bacterial and gives out red fluorescence [5], and the fluorescence images of E. coli being treated with 3% CQDs/MoO3-x composite is shown in Fig. 8 . The cells show strong green fluorescence at 0 min (Fig. 8a). An increase of bacteria with red fluorescence were observed, indicating that the integrity of cell membrane was gradually lost during the photocatalytic antibacterial process (Fig. 8b-d). After 40 min, the fluorescence was red, indicating that the catalyst may destroy all bacteria (Fig. 8e). This may be due to the early death of living cells and structural damage caused by catalysts [53]. The results show that the photocatalyst could damage all bacteria after 40 min of LED lamp irradiation in water.

The light absorption performance, the transfer efficiency of photo-excited carriers and active species are main factors in the photocatalytic system. These factors can be detected by UV–vis DRS, EIS and capture experiment, respectively. The light absorption property of MoO3, MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was measured by using UV–vis DRS (Fig. 9 a). For MoO3, the light absorption range is from 200 to 420 nm, which originated from the response of charge transfer from the valence band to the conduction band. Compared with MoO3, MoO3-x show a wider and stronger light absorption range from 200 to 800 nm due to its narrow band gap induced by oxygen vacancy [40]. In the case of 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, much stronger light absorption from 200 to 800 nm was observed. Therefore, in CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, both oxygen vacancy and CQDs make a good function for the light absorption property, which is instrumental in absorbing more photons and leading an enhanced photocatalytic activity. The repeatability of the photocatalyst was studied by using 3% CQDs/MoO3-x to inactivate E. coli. In three experiments, the antibacterial performance of the sample was still maintained as the original level (Fig. S6). The result indicated that 3% CQDs/MoO3-x composite had good stability.

Meanwhile, it is known that UV–vis absorption spectra can provide information that related to the optical properties and band gap of semiconductor. Then, the band-gap energy (E
g ) of the photocatalyst can be calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
α h
v = A
(
h v
-
E g
)
1 2
where α is absorption coefficient, hν is incident photon energy, A is a constant, respectively [54]. The E
g of MoO3-x was obtained as 2.40 eV (Fig. 9b). In addition, in the electrochemical Mott-Schottky plot (MS-plot) of MoO3-x (Fig. 9c), the flat band potential was about −0.55 V (versus Ag/AgCl electrode, pH = 7), which is corresponding to +0.31 V (versus NHE) [55]. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of MoO3-x semiconductor were determined to be 0.31 V and 2.71 V (vs., NHE) according to the formula E
CB = E
VB - E
g [56,57] from MS-plot (Fig. 9c) where an estimated flat potential was directly used as the conduction band potential [58].

In view of above discussion, the introduction of CQDs may reduce recombination efficiency of the electrons and holes, enhance charge separation of MoO3-x and improve the photocatalytic activity. To confirm this idea, EIS of pure MoO3, MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was taken to detect the efficiency of electron transfer (Fig. 10 a). The arc-radius of EIS indicates the charge transfer efficiency of the material at the contact interface between the working electrode and the electrolyte solution. The smaller radius means the faster charge transfer rate [59,60]. It can be observed that the radius of MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x is much smaller than MoO3, which is attributed to the oxygen vacancy in MoO3-x. To further investigate the details between MoO3-x and 3% CQDs/MoO3-x, the enlarged spectra were shown in Fig. 10b (from Fig. 10a). It can be observed that the arc radius of 3% CQDs/MoO3-x is smaller than MoO3-x, indicating a more efficient charge transfer by the introduction of CQDs [61]. In sum up, for 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst, a faster interfacial charge transfer and more efficient separation of electron-hole pairs were obtained, subsequently resulting in an enhancement of photocatalytic performance.

To understand the active species in this photocatalytic system, a capture experiment was taken on 3% CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst (Fig. 10c). The exact roles of active species were detected by several scavengers, which were triethanolamine (TEOA, 1 mM) for h+, tert-Butanol (t-BuOH, 1 mM) for •OH and the inhibitor agent N2 for •O2 −. Before conducting the experiment, the concentration of every scavenger was ensured the maximum scavenging effect, which caused no inactivation to the bacteria [11]. When the t-BuOH was put in the system, the inactivation efficiency of E. coli and S. aureus was hardly decreased, suggesting that •OH did not play an important role. However, when TEOA was added, the inactivation efficiency of E. coli and S. aureus greatly decreased, implying h+ is the main reactive species in the photocatalytic process.

Based on above analysis, the photocatalytic antibacterial process of the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst under LED light irradiation could be explained (Fig. 11 ). Due to the good adsorption ability of visible light, when CQDs/MoO3-x was illuminated by LED light, electron-hole pairs can be excited from the MoO3-x owing to its narrow band-gap (around 2.4 eV). For the good electrical conductivity of CQDs [37,62], the photogenerated electrons can freely move to the surface of CQDs layer, and the holes was left in the valence band of MoO3-x [46]. The recombination of electron-hole pairs was inhibited, and the number of holes was increased and they are easy to attack E. coli and S. aureus, leading to the destruction of the bacterial cell membrane. Therefore, the CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst showed good photocatalytic antibacterial performance.

A novel CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst was facilely prepared by a hydrothermal route. UV–vis spectra indicated CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst had better visible light absorption property. EIS result confirmed there was a more efficient charge transfer in 3% CQDs/MoO3-x than in MoO3-x. Antibacterial tests showed CQDs/MoO3-x photocatalyst can more effectively inactivate E. coli and S. aureus compared with pure MoO3-x. The optimum sample 3% CQDs/MoO3-x can thoroughly inactivate E. coli and S. aureus in 40 min. Based on capture experiments, h+ played an important role in the antibacterial system. The combination of 1D structure MoO3-x and CQDs not only reinforced light absorption but also largely improved the photogenerated electron-hole separation, leading to an enhanced photoactivity. The interesting result presented a potential way for further utilizing MoO3-x-based photocatalyst for water sterilization.

Yeping Li: Writing - review & editing. Chaobao Wang: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Fei Zhang: Data curation. Hao Wang: Data curation. Qian Wang: Data curation. Lei Yang: Investigation. Meng Xie: Writing - review & editing. Yu Gan: Investigation. Huaming Li: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: National Nature Science Foundation of China (21406094)

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors genuinely appreciate the financial support of this work from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (21406094).

ce:section.ce:para:
